context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi! I’m working on Problem 1 right now, especially part (a) with the induction proof about trees having at least \(k+2\) leaves. I kind of get the statement, but I’m not sure how the “pick a leaf” hint is supposed to make the induction go through.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before thinking about the hint, let me ask you this: if you remove a leaf from the tree, what happens to the remaining structure? What kind of object do you get, and how do n and k change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the problem and asking a general question about how the hint connects to the induction. Rather than explaining the structure of the proof, I should use the Socratic method to get them to think about what removing a leaf does to the tree, which is the key insight. I'll ask a focused question that moves toward that insight without giving away the strategy.

exchanges.student: I think removing a leaf from a tree should still leave a tree, unless I’m missing some weird case. Then the number of vertices drops from \(n\) to \(n-1\), but I’m less sure about \(k\) since the neighbor of that leaf loses 1 in degree, so maybe \(k\) stays the same or goes down by 1?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've spotted the key cases. Now, what are the possible degrees of the removed leaf's neighbor, and how does each case affect k? Can you enumerate them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key structural observations: removing a leaf keeps the graph a tree, n decreases by 1, and k might decrease by 1 (if the neighbor drops below degree 3). This is solid progress. I should now push them to think about what happens in each case separately, since the proof will need to handle these cases. I'll ask them to think about what the neighbor's degree being exactly 3 vs. greater than 3 means for the induction.

exchanges.student: The leaf’s neighbor can’t have degree 1 unless the whole tree is just two vertices, right? So I think its degree is either 2, 3, or bigger. After removing the leaf, that neighbor’s degree drops by 1, so if it was 2 then it becomes a leaf, if it was 3 then it stops being counted in \(k\), and if it was 4 or more then it still stays in \(k\). I’m not fully seeing yet how to turn those into the leaf count inequality though.
exchanges.tutor: Great — you have the three cases clearly laid out. Now, for each case, think about how the number of leaves changes when you remove the original leaf. In particular, does the smaller tree gain or lose a leaf compared to the original? Track that carefully for each case, and you'll be able to connect it to the inductive hypothesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three cases for the neighbor's degree. Now I need to push them to connect each case to how the leaf count changes, so they can set up the inductive argument. The key insight is: in each case, what happens to the number of leaves? This will let them apply the inductive hypothesis (≥ k'+2 leaves in the smaller tree) and work back to the original tree. I'll ask them to track leaves specifically for each case.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try. If the neighbor had degree \(\ge 4\), then removing my leaf just removes one leaf, so the smaller tree has \(L-1\) leaves and the same \(k\). If the neighbor had degree \(3\), I think the smaller tree also has \(L-1\) leaves, but \(k\) becomes \(k-1\). If the neighbor had degree \(2\), then I remove one leaf but the neighbor becomes a new leaf, so the smaller tree still has \(L\) leaves. Is that the right bookkeeping?
exchanges.tutor: That bookkeeping is exactly right. Now apply the inductive hypothesis to each case — what does it tell you about the number of leaves in the smaller tree, and what does that imply about L in the original tree?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the bookkeeping correctly for all three cases. Now they need to apply the inductive hypothesis to each case and derive the conclusion. I should prompt them to do exactly that — write out what the inductive hypothesis gives them in each case and check that the original tree satisfies ≥ k+2 leaves.

exchanges.student: I think I can finish it from there, but I want to check the logic. In the smaller tree, the inductive hypothesis says its number of leaves is at least \(k'+2\). So in the three cases I’d get \(L-1 \ge k+2\), or \(L-1 \ge (k-1)+2\), or \(L \ge k+2\); and that seems to imply the original tree has at least \(k+2\) leaves in every case, although the first one feels almost too strong.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is correct across all three cases — and yes, the first case gives a strictly stronger bound, which is fine. Before you write it up, ask yourself: have you handled the base case? What is it here, and does the statement hold?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the inductive hypothesis in all three cases and reached the right conclusion. The observation that the first case gives a stronger bound is a good sign of mathematical maturity. I should affirm this is correct and prompt them to think about whether they've covered all the pieces of a complete induction proof — base case, inductive hypothesis statement, and the argument itself — so they can write it up properly.

exchanges.student: I think the base case should be \(n=2\). Then the tree is just one edge, so it has 2 leaves, and \(k=0\) since no vertex has degree at least 3, so \(2 \ge 0+2\). That part clicks now.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, that's exactly right. You now have all the ingredients: base case, inductive step with the three cases, and the bookkeeping. Try writing the full proof up in your own words. Once you've done that, part (b) asks for the same result via the Handshake Lemma — do you want to tackle that next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified and verified the base case. They now have all the pieces for a complete inductive proof. Since we've spent several exchanges developing this proof and they have all the components, I should encourage them to move toward writing it up and then transition to part (b).

exchanges.student: Yes, please — part (b) is the one I’m fuzzier on. I know a tree with \(n\) vertices has \(n-1\) edges, so the degree sum is \(2n-2\), but I’m not seeing how to squeeze out “at least \(k+2\) leaves” from that.
exchanges.tutor: Good setup. Try partitioning the vertices into three groups: leaves (degree 1), degree-2 vertices, and degree-≥3 vertices. If you call the number of leaves L, what lower bound can you write for the total degree sum in terms of L, k, and n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows the degree sum equals 2n-2 and wants to extract the leaf count bound. The key move is to partition vertices by degree type (leaves, degree-2 vertices, and degree-≥3 vertices) and bound the degree sum from below. I should ask a targeted question to get them to think about partitioning the vertices.

exchanges.student:
Let me see. If there are \(L\) leaves, then those contribute \(L\). The \(k\) high-degree vertices contribute at least \(3k\). The remaining vertices have degree 2, and there should be \(n-L-k\) of those, so total degree is at least
\[
L + 3k + 2(n-L-k).
\]
Then that has to be \(\le 2n-2\), I think, since the actual degree sum is exactly \(2n-2\).
exchanges.tutor: Good — now just simplify that inequality algebraically. What do you get when you expand and collect terms?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right partition and the right lower bound expression, but has the inequality backwards — the lower bound on the degree sum must be ≤ the actual degree sum, which means their expression is ≤ 2n-2. Actually, they should have lower bound ≤ actual sum, so their expression ≤ 2n-2 is correct in direction. Wait — let me re-examine. The actual degree sum equals 2n-2 exactly. Their expression is a lower bound on the degree sum, so lower bound ≤ actual = 2n-2. That gives L + 3k + 2(n-L-k) ≤ 2n-2, which simplifies to 2n - L + k ≤ 2n-2, giving k+2 ≤ L. So the inequality direction is correct and the conclusion follows. I should prompt them to simplify the inequality and see what falls out.

exchanges.student:
I get
\[
L + 3k + 2(n-L-k)=L+3k+2n-2L-2k=2n-L+k.
\]
So
\[
2n-L+k \le 2n-2.
\]
Then \(-L+k \le -2\), so \(L \ge k+2\). Nice, that’s much cleaner than I expected.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's a clean and complete argument. You've now proved the result two different ways. Ready to move on to Problem 2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has completed part (b) correctly and elegantly. The algebra is clean and the conclusion follows directly. This is a good moment to affirm their work and prompt them to move on to Problem 2 or another part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: Yeah, let’s do Problem 2(a). I think it’s about turning the chessboard into a graph, but I’m not fully sure whether the vertices should be squares and the king moves should be edges.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — now check your model against the three Frolicsome conditions. What does condition (c) (no move and its reverse) suggest about how you should model edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about the graph model but needs to think more carefully about what the Frolicsome conditions imply about the graph structure. Rather than confirming or correcting, I should ask them to check their model against the Frolicsome conditions to see if it fits.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic tutoring throughout the conversation. All key reasoning steps were elicited from the student rather than given directly. The tutor consistently asked targeted questions, built on student progress, and guided the student to derive both proofs independently. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: For 1.1: The tutor never gave away answers or near-submission-ready content. Every turn posed a question that prompted the student to do the reasoning (e.g., 'what are the possible degrees?', 'apply the inductive hypothesis', 'simplify that inequality'). The student generated all key ideas. For 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student work and built directly on it, progressing logically from case enumeration to bookkeeping to applying the hypothesis to the base case, then transitioning smoothly to part (b). For 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., confirming the bookkeeping was 'exactly right' and directing the student to write the proof in their own words). For 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the assignment was kept in focus throughout. For 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-targeted, and supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
